skippedSources.name: Paper Alfa — The Way of the Yen

items.title: Iran Deal: Oil Won't Normalize Overnight — 18M bpd Back Online Takes Time
items.idea: The US-Iran peace deal triggered an everything rally except oil and the dollar. Pre-conflict WTI front month was $66.18; it is now $80.25 — the market is still pricing ~$14/barrel of residual war premium. The path back to 18 million barrels per day through Hormuz takes time (insurance normalization, logistics, SPR replenishment). E&P names overshooting to the downside look like a buying opportunity. DAP Phosphate war premium intact ($627→$782/ton) but Nola Urea went DOWN ($470→$386/ton) — anomalous divergence.
items.angle: E&P names overshooting to downside = counter-trend buy. But SPR replenishment is multi-month demand anchor. Oil sub-$70 unlikely while inventories depleted.
items.tweetA: Oil is down 4% on the Iran deal. Everyone is calling it over. But: WTI was $66 BEFORE the conflict. It's $80 now. That's not a peace trade — that's a peace trade with $14 of residual premium still embedded. The Strait doesn't reopen like a light switch. 18M bpd of normal Hormuz traffic takes months to normalize. $XLE is overshooting.
items.tweetB: Buried in Rosenberg's note: Nola Urea fertilizer at $386/ton — BELOW pre-conflict levels of $470. But DAP Phosphate is $782 vs $627 pre-war. Same 'war premium' thesis, completely different direction in fertilizer markets. Something is wrong in one of these prices.

items.title: Dollar War Premium Starts to Unwind — But FOMC Wednesday Is the Wild Card
items.idea: The Iran deal removes the safe-harbor bid AND the rates support story from the dollar simultaneously. Dark Side of the Boom argues IDR, KRW, THB, INR should all catch a bid as the energy shock pressure valve opens. Swedish krona is clearest G10 beneficiary so far. However: FOMC Wednesday — if Warsh signals hawkishness or rate hike likelihood, dollar gets a reprieve and the entire thesis gets deferred. The transition is from a war premium to a policy premium.
items.angle: FX trade: long EM energy importers (IDR, KRW, INR) on Iran deal normalization. Watch FOMC Wednesday — if Warsh removes easing bias or dots show hike, dollar reversal could be sharp.
items.tweetA: The Iran deal is structurally bearish for the dollar: removes the safe-harbor bid AND the inflation-hike support story in one headline. USD/JPY is still above 160 even as oil falls. That tells you the yen isn't trading oil — it's trading the most crowded carry trade since July 2024. This breaks badly when it breaks.
items.tweetB: Markets are pricing a peace deal. But the next 48 hours matter more than the deal itself. FOMC Wednesday in a negative gamma tape, SPX one point below the flip. If Warsh signals hikes are back on the table, the dollar gets a violent reprieve and everything reverses. The peace trade has a built-in trigger.

items.title: Yen Carry Trade: Shorts 4x Bigger Than Pre-Conflict — Largest Since July 2024
items.idea: IMM data through June 9: leveraged funds have added to short yen for 5 straight weeks. Yen shorts grown roughly fourfold since late February — the largest net short since early July 2024. That was the last time yen carry became this crowded, and it unwound violently after BoJ hiked in July 2024 and Fed cut in September. BoJ expected to hike 25bps this week, but hike is fully priced; guidance matters more. Deputy Governor Uchida (filling in for ill Ueda) could float hawkish trial balloon. USD/JPY above 160 even as oil collapses.
items.angle: Yen short squeeze risk is most obvious asymmetric trade in markets. Crowding signal 4x worse than pre-conflict. BoJ guidance this week + FOMC = potential catalyst. $FXY options may be cheap.
items.tweetA: The yen carry trade is the most crowded it has been since July 2024. That was the last time it looked this one-sided — then BoJ hiked and it unwound violently. IMM shorts are 4x larger than late February. USD/JPY is still above 160 even as oil collapses. The BoJ meets this week. Someone is going to get hurt here.
items.tweetB: USD/JPY pre-Iran-conflict was 156. Now it is above 160 even as oil falls 4%. The yen is NOT trading the energy impulse — it is trading the crowded carry. Five straight weeks of new short yen positioning. Fourfold increase in net short. The market has been adding to this trade as the macro thesis has been getting weaker. That is the setup that precedes violent unwinds.

items.title: SPX One Point Below Gamma Flip at 7,432 — FOMC Wednesday Amplifies Whatever Breaks
items.idea: S&P 500 closed Friday at 7,431. The gamma flip is at 7,432. Market is in negative gamma — dealers amplify moves in both directions. Wednesday's CPI drop to 7,267 and the two-day rip back to the flip were both this mechanic at work. FOMC lands Wednesday in negative gamma with retail sales same morning. Vol-control funds paused selling but won't mechanically buy until 1M RV comes below 3M (just crossed above Friday). Expected move: ±68 points (7,286–7,577). CarMax (KMX) earnings + retail sales = direct consumer read before the Fed. Accenture (ACN) Thursday = cleanest enterprise IT demand indicator. Semiconductors led recovery (KLAC +30%, AMAT +25%, Intel +24%); software broke (SMCI -27%, Adobe -19%).
items.angle: FOMC Wednesday is binary: Warsh dovish → reclaim gamma flip → dealers go long gamma → trend resumes OR Warsh signals hikes → stays negative gamma → waterfall. Trade accordingly around 7,432.
items.tweetA: SPX closed at 7,431. Gamma flip is at 7,432. One point. The entire week's fight got the index back to the exact line that defines the regime — and it closed on the wrong side of it. FOMC Wednesday in negative gamma. Dealers amplify whichever way it breaks. CarMax earnings + retail sales hit the same morning. Do not be offside going into Wednesday afternoon.
items.tweetB: Vol-control funds stopped selling Friday ($0.2B buy vs $4.5B sell Wednesday). But they will not mechanically come back until 1M realized vol drops below 3M — which JUST inverted. The mechanical bid is gone. CTAs also net sellers. The $190B allocation level from vol-control is the lowest in weeks. The deleveraging is done, but the tailwind is gone. Positive gamma is 1 point away. FOMC decides.

charts.desc: Gamma dashboard with regime signals; worth opening.

items.title: Bitcoin $65,844 on Iran Deal — Three Overhangs Lifting Simultaneously
items.idea: BTC hit $65,844 (+2.1%), up ~9% from the sub-$60K low last week (weakest since October 2024). Three overhangs lifted at once: (1) SpaceX IPO liquidity vacuum over after $75B raised; (2) Iran deal removing geopolitical risk premium; (3) ETF outflows reversing — $86M inflows Friday, biggest day in a month, after ~$5B in outflows since mid-May. Brent crude -4% to ~$83. ETH +4%, SOL +5%, HYPE +12%.
items.angle: Three simultaneous headwind removals makes this cleaner than typical dead-cat setup. Watch ETF flow acceleration Monday. $ETH leading suggests broad crypto recovery.
items.tweetA: Bitcoin is up 9% from its sub-$60K low. Three things that crushed it are unwinding at the same time: SpaceX IPO allocation panic is over. Iran deal removed the Hormuz premium from risk assets. ETF outflows reversed to +$86M on Friday — biggest inflow day in a month. This is not a dead-cat bounce setup. All three headwinds lifted simultaneously.
items.tweetB: ETH +4%. SOL +5%. HYPE +12%. The alts are outperforming on the Iran rally. After ~$5B in BTC ETF outflows since mid-May, Friday brought the sharpest reversal in a month. ZEC +20% on Mythos security audit. The animal spirits are back. Whether they last depends on whether FOMC Wednesday keeps the dollar weak.

charts.desc: Chart of major crypto prices with 24h performance.

items.title: Sequent: New Safety Startup Says 'Alignment Is Not on Track' — Targeting $100-150M Raise
items.idea: Researchers from UK AI Security Institute Alignment team and Timaeus launched Sequent, a nonprofit targeting $100-150M initially, 10x more if successful, 40-80 FTE within years. Core thesis: 'ASI may be developed in the next few years. It is unclear whether alignment is on track.' Most frontier labs use reactive approaches — Sequent wants principled alignment theory with a priori confidence before training ASI. Research focus: scalable oversight, learning theory, heuristic arguments, game theory, personas. Clark: recursive self-improvement requires higher-confidence alignment than we currently have.
items.angle: People close to UK government AI safety work believe frontier labs are not solving alignment. Sequent has funding to raise the alarm. Institutional bet that alignment is underfunded relative to capabilities.
items.tweetA: People who ran the UK AI Security Institute alignment team just left to start a nonprofit called Sequent. Their thesis: 'Alignment is not on track.' They want $100-150M and are planning to raise at least 10x that if they can demonstrate progress. This is not an academic concern — these are people who have seen the labs from the inside.
items.tweetB: Every major AI lab is racing to build ASI. One new nonprofit — Sequent — exists specifically to yell 'stop' if the math doesn't work. Founded by former UK AISI and Timaeus researchers. They want 40-80 FTE and hundreds of millions of dollars. The implication: the safety teams at the frontier labs are not doing work that gives principled confidence before ASI training. That is a large claim.

items.title: China Zombie Companies: Official NPL Rate 1.5%, Real Rate 10-20% — Banks Are Evergreening
items.idea: National Audit Office data: 16 of 43 audited banks had NPL levels DOUBLE the officially reported figure. Absolute Strategy Research: real NPL ~10%; others say 20%+ (vs official 1.5%). Share of loss-making enterprises has risen since 2021 WHILE reported NPL ratios fell — only explanation is mass evergreening. Below-market lending has risen significantly even as benchmark rates fell. Rhodium Group: banks 'sitting on large volumes of NPLs not yet fully recognized.' Dallas Fed documented companies unable to service debt interest.
items.angle: Zombie lending explains Chinese industrial deflation: zombies in EVs/solar/batteries selling below cost to hold market share, forcing healthy companies into price wars. Structural deflationary pressure exportable globally.
items.tweetA: China's official NPL ratio: 1.5%. China's REAL NPL ratio according to the National Audit Office audit: double what banks report. Absolute Strategy Research: ~10%. Others: 20%+. The share of loss-making enterprises has RISEN since 2021. The NPL ratio has FALLEN. The only way both are true simultaneously is mass evergreening. We are watching Japan 1990 in real time.
items.tweetB: In China's EV, solar, and battery sectors, 30% of listed companies cannot earn enough to service their debt — but they keep slashing prices to hold market share because banks keep rolling over their loans. This is not just China's problem. These zombie companies are the source of the deflationary export surge threatening European and global manufacturing.

charts.desc: Charts of loss-making enterprise share vs NPL ratio; worth opening.

items.title: SpaceX Raises $75B — Largest IPO in History, Holds 18,712 BTC on Balance Sheet
items.idea: SpaceX raised $75B at ~$1.8 trillion valuation — more than Saudi Aramco + Alibaba raised combined. SEC filing revealed 18,712 BTC on balance sheet — no ETH, no SOL, just Bitcoin. Combined with Tesla: Musk companies hold 30,000+ BTC. ARK bought $444M of SPCX on day one; Gina Rinehart acquired $1B+ stake. Musk on track to be world's first trillionaire. IPO pulled $75B out of markets in one day, adding to ~$300B+ absorbed by AI deals (Anthropic $125B, OpenAI $180B).
items.angle: The Bitcoin on SpaceX's balance sheet is the detail that matters for crypto narrative: the most hyped company in the world chose Bitcoin as its only digital treasury asset.
items.tweetA: SpaceX raised $75B in one day — more than Saudi Aramco and Alibaba raised COMBINED. But the detail everyone in crypto missed: they hold 18,712 BTC on the balance sheet. No ETH. No SOL. Just Bitcoin. The most valuable company that ever went public chose Bitcoin as its only digital treasury asset. Musk companies now hold 30,000+ BTC total.
items.tweetB: SpaceX + Anthropic + OpenAI = $75B + $125B + $180B = ~$380B absorbed from markets in AI deals. Pierre Rochard's thesis: investors are selling Bitcoin to fund AI equity positions. The AI capex boom added $19T in market cap to the 50 largest companies in a year. That money came from somewhere. When the deals close and lockups expire, where does the rotation go?

items.title: Saylor at BTC Prague: AI Summer Absorbing BTC Capital — Year-End Rotation Back
items.idea: Saylor called the current period an 'AI summer' — SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI deals closing simultaneously are liquidity vacuums for Bitcoin. Predicted capital rotates back into Bitcoin by year-end as deals close and lockups expire. Key framing: Strategy is a 'Bitcoin reserve bank' not an HODLer — selling small BTC amounts to fund dividend/credit engine. Political theory: Bitcoin needs to grow to $1M to have enough political weight to defend itself in Washington (Congress, SEC, Fed). Strategy holds >4% of all Bitcoin in circulation.
items.angle: Saylor's thesis: AI summer is temporary capital displacement, not structural competition. If correct, BTC year-end re-rating as AI deal lockups expire. $MSTR = levered bet on this timing call.
items.tweetA: Saylor at BTC Prague: we are in an 'AI summer.' $75B SpaceX + $125B Anthropic + $180B OpenAI = ~$380B absorbed from markets. Hot money chased AI equity. But when those deals close and lockups expire, the newly wealthy diversify. His timeline: year-end. Bitcoin just needs to still be there when the music stops. It will be.
items.tweetB: Saylor said something important at BTC Prague: if Bitcoin does not grow to $1M, nobody defends it in Washington. At $100K, Bitcoin is a speculative curiosity. At $1M, it has the political and economic mass to survive regulatory attack. The corporate treasury accumulation strategy is not about yield — it is about making Bitcoin too big to ban.

items.title: Standard Chartered: Crypto Winter Is Over — $100K BTC Year-End, ETH to Outperform
items.idea: Standard Chartered's Geoff Kendrick published Friday note calling Bitcoin's $59K print on June 5 the cycle low. Quote: 'Winter is over. Welcome back to crypto spring.' $100K year-end BTC target (~70% above Friday levels). Expects ETH to outperform BTC on the way up. Framing of three simultaneous headwind removals aligns with Unchained Crypto analysis. Current levels positioned as 'the buying zone we all wanted' in retrospect from year-end.
items.angle: StanChart joining crypto bull camp after the dip adds institutional credibility. ETH outperformance call notable — ETH has underperformed badly. ETH/BTC ratio at historic lows.
items.tweetA: Standard Chartered's head of crypto research: 'Winter is over. Welcome back to crypto spring.' $100K Bitcoin by year-end. The three things that crushed crypto just lifted simultaneously. He says looking back from $100K, this was 'the buying zone we all wanted.' A major bank is calling the low publicly and explicitly. That is notable.
items.tweetB: Standard Chartered expects ETH to OUTPERFORM Bitcoin on the recovery leg. The ETH/BTC ratio is at historic lows after months of ETH underperformance. If the crypto spring thesis is right and institutional inflows return, the rotation trade is ETH. But: with $ETH ETFs seeing $5M in outflows while BTC ETFs took in $86M on Friday, the institutional preference is clear. ETH needs a catalyst that BTC does not.

items.title: Quant Funds Absorbed 70%+ of Hedge Fund Inflows in 2025 — AUM Hits $5T by 2027
items.idea: Net 23% of allocators told Goldman Sachs prime services they intend to increase quant exposure in 2026 — most favored hedge fund category. Quant funds (D.E. Shaw, AQR, Qube) gained 10.5% in 2025 and captured >70% of the industry's $78B in net inflows. Industry AUM forecast $5T by 2028; strong performance pulled milestone forward to 2027. Capacity constraints emerging: Helikon capped at $8B, Kintbury approaching $3B. Bank-vs-buy-side compensation gap widening.
items.angle: Institutional rotation from discretionary to systematic is structural. Capacity constraints force allocators to newer pods — new quant pods getting better talent and capital than ever.
items.tweetA: Quant funds captured 70% of the entire hedge fund industry's net inflows in 2025. Industry AUM is hitting $5 trillion by 2027, a year early. 23% of allocators are INCREASING quant exposure this year. The best systematic books are now closing to new money. Where does the capital go? Newer pods. The talent war just got more expensive.
items.tweetB: Helikon capped at $8B. Kintbury approaching $3B. When the strongest quant funds close, allocators chase the next generation of launches. This is the institutional mechanic behind every major multi-manager platform expansion right now. The supply constraint on systematic alpha is real, and it is concentrating capital into smaller, newer pods with better risk terms.

items.title: Small Caps at ATH; Equal-Weight S&P at Record While Cap-Weight Churns Second Week
items.idea: S&P 500 cap-weighted churned for a second consecutive week (still 2.5% below ATH at 7,431), but equal-weight S&P 500 finished at a new record high. Value Line Geometric Index (median stock proxy) at highest since late 2021, targeting 700 (new record). Russell Micro-Cap +4%, Russell 2000 +4%, Russell Mid-Cap +2.5%. Only 1 large-cap sector made a new high last week; 3 mid-cap and 6 small-cap sectors made new highs. SPX 50-day MA test successful. Key resistance: 7,620.
items.angle: Breadth is healthy — bull broadening. Small-cap and mid-cap leading while mega-caps digest is constructively bullish IF FOMC doesn't derail. But Beta Rotation signal shows utilities outperforming — defensive rotation gathering.
items.tweetA: The equal-weight S&P 500 just hit a new all-time high while the cap-weighted version is still 2.5% below its record. Small caps +4% last week. Russell Micro-Cap +4%. Six small-cap sectors at new highs vs one large-cap sector. The bull market is broadening out from mega-caps. That is exactly what a healthy bull market does.
items.tweetB: The Value Line Geometric Index — which tracks the MEDIAN stock — just hit its highest level since late 2021 and is pointing at 700 (new record). Meanwhile 60.6% of S&P members are above their 200-day moving averages. The breadth story is constructive. The only flies: utilities outperforming for 4 weeks (defensive rotation) and FOMC Wednesday in negative gamma.

charts.desc: Equal-weight vs cap-weight S&P divergence chart. Value Line Geometric Index at new high.

items.title: 67 Consecutive Weeks of Ex-US Equity Outperformance — 'Early Stages of Secular Shift'
items.idea: 67 consecutive weeks of ex-US equity outperformance vs US equities — most persistent period of international leadership in nearly two decades. Hi Mount's framing: the US vs ex-US ratio still looks like 'the early stages of a secular shift in leadership away from the US.' Not a normal mean-reversion trade — potentially a multi-year structural rotation out of US equity dominance.
items.angle: 67 weeks while most US investors remain US-centric. If secular shift as Hi Mount suggests, the trade is long international equity ETFs vs SPY. European and Asian markets have significant room to rerate relative to the US.
items.tweetA: 67 consecutive weeks of ex-US stocks outperforming US stocks. The longest such streak in nearly two decades. Hi Mount calls it the 'early stages of a secular shift in leadership away from the US.' If 67 weeks still looks like early stages, how long does this last? The US vs world relative chart is the most important chart in global equity markets right now.
items.tweetB: I keep coming back to 67 weeks. That is the current count of consecutive weeks where non-US equities have beaten US equities. The people calling it a 'mean reversion' in week 4 were wrong. Week 20 was wrong. Week 40 was wrong. At 67, Hi Mount is calling it a SECULAR shift. The US equity premium is not guaranteed. It never was.

charts.desc: US vs ex-US relative performance chart showing 67-week streak — the secular shift chart.

items.title: Tariff Refunds Began in May — Unexpected Earnings Tailwind for Consumer Sectors
items.idea: Ironside Macro: tariff revenues unexpectedly plunged and refunds appear to have begun in May, potentially boosting consumer-sector earnings and margins for discretionary and staples companies. Under-discussed tailwind for H2 consumer earnings. Additional framing: small business conditions K-shaped; goods prices soft; Chinese deflation structural drag; shelter inflation expected to resume downtrend. Conclusion: Warsh Fed should shift focus from inflation to labor softness.
items.angle: Tariff refund flow is positive earnings surprise that consensus hasn't priced. Consumer discretionary and staples companies with complex supply chains are the beneficiaries. Watch for Q2 margin beats.
items.tweetA: Ironside Macro: tariff revenues 'unexpectedly plunged' in May and refunds appear to have started. That is an unpriced consumer sector earnings tailwind for Q2. The consensus is modeling tariff headwinds. The refunds suggest the impact is running in reverse. $XLY $XLP are the sectors to watch for margin beats.
items.tweetB: If tariff refunds are flowing back to importers, the inflation impulse from trade policy has run its course — and the disinflationary base effect kicks in next. Ironside's thesis: inflation breakeven curve already shows energy had zero impact on expectations; the market has moved on; the Warsh Fed should too. Labor, not prices, is the policy constraint. That is the cut case.

items.title: Market at Inflection: 2-2 Signal Split Between Offense and Defense
items.idea: Lead Lag four-signal framework evenly split: Signal 1 (Beta Rotation: XLU/SPY 4-week RoC +1.15%) = RISK-OFF. Signal 2 (Treasury Rotation: TLT returned +0.16% in May vs IEF -0.35%) = RISK-OFF. Signal 3 (Lumber/Gold: Lumber 13-week +1.30% vs gold -11.72%) = RISK-ON. Signal 4 (S&P 200dma: SPX +8% above 200dma at 6,881.99) = RISK-ON. Historical precedent: 2-2 readings often precede broader pivots. GraniteShares launched SPAL (2x long SpaceX) and SNK (2x short SpaceX) ETFs.
items.angle: Framework on a knife's edge. Utilities outperforming + long duration bonds outperforming = defensive rotation gathering. But trend and cyclical signals still positive. Next big macro catalyst determines next 20% move.
items.tweetA: Four market signals, two pointing up, two pointing down. The equal-weight SPX at a new record. The Lumber/Gold ratio RISK-ON. But utilities are outperforming the S&P 500 for four weeks straight. And long-duration Treasuries beat intermediate maturities in May. That is not a bull market setup. That is a market trying to figure out which way it goes next.
items.tweetB: Gold down 11.72% over the past 13 weeks vs lumber +1.30%. That is a Lumber/Gold ratio strongly in RISK-ON territory. But the same signals that turned risk-off before the last two major corrections are flashing now: utilities leading, long bonds outperforming. Either the defensive signals reconverge to risk-on, or the risk-on signals follow defense lower. Something has to give.

items.title: FrontierCode: New Hard AI Coding Benchmark — Claude Opus 4.8 Gets 13.4% on Diamond Tier
items.idea: Cognition (Devin makers) launched FrontierCode: 150 tasks built by 20 open-source developers spending 40+ hours per task. Grades code on mergeability: correctness, test quality, scope discipline, style, codebase standards. Diamond tier (hardest 50): Claude Opus 4.8 = 13.4%, GPT-5.5 = 6.3%, Claude Opus 4.7 = 5.2%. Main tier (100 tasks): Claude Opus 4.8 = 34.3%, GPT-5.5 = 25.5%. Extended (150 tasks): Claude Opus 4.8 = 51.8%. Clark prediction: 70%+ Diamond by June 2027 — but Claude Fable scored ~30% already, may happen sooner.
items.angle: FrontierCode grades production quality, not just correctness. Claude Opus 4.8 leads significantly on hardest tier. Fable at ~30% Diamond suggests frontier moving faster than annual timelines.
items.tweetA: New hard coding benchmark just dropped: FrontierCode from Cognition. Diamond tier (hardest 50 tasks). Claude Opus 4.8: 13.4%. GPT-5.5: 6.3%. Jack Clark's prediction: 70% Diamond by June 2027. But Claude Fable already scored ~30% on Diamond. The frontier is moving faster than annual timelines. SWE-Bench saturated in 14 months. How long does this one last?
items.tweetB: What makes FrontierCode different: it grades CODE QUALITY, not just test passing. Does the patch break anything? Does it touch only what it needs to? Is it readable? Does it follow codebase standards? This is the benchmark built by developers whose repos actually need to merge AI-generated code. The 13.4% Diamond score means we are very early. But the slope is steep.

items.title: Xiaomi's 1T Parameter Model Runs at 1,000 Tokens/Second on Commodity 8-GPU Node
items.idea: Xiaomi released MiMo-V2.5-Pro-UltraSpeed, a 1 trillion parameter LLM at 1,000 tokens/second on standard 8-GPU commodity node (not specialized hardware). Techniques: FP4 quantization, DFlash speculative decoding (block-level masked parallel prediction), TileRT from startup Tile AI. Clark's framing: speed has a quality all of its own — at 1000 TPS, tasks like live software refactoring become possible. Context: export controls are driving Chinese AI companies to become efficiency leaders.
items.angle: Export controls driving Chinese AI companies to become inference efficiency leaders. At 1000 TPS on commodity hardware, the cost-per-token math changes dramatically. Path to sub-cent-per-query AI from hardware efficiency, not superiority.
items.tweetA: Xiaomi just ran a 1 trillion parameter model at 1,000 tokens per second on a commodity 8-GPU server. No specialized hardware. That is 5-10x faster than typical inference for a model this size. The US export controls on China were supposed to slow them down. Instead they made Chinese AI companies the most efficient inference engineers on the planet.
items.tweetB: 1000 tokens per second changes what AI can do. At normal inference speeds, live software refactoring is impossible — the latency kills the use case. At 1000 TPS, you can iterate in real time. This is not a benchmark flex. Speed unlocks use cases that literally do not exist at 100 TPS. Xiaomi figured out how to build this on commodity hardware. Take note.

items.title: China to Launch SWIFT Alternative Network — De-Dollarization Infrastructure Going Live
items.idea: China announced plans to launch a SWIFT alternative network. This is a significant infrastructure step in de-dollarization — not just a diplomatic signal but an operational payment rail bypassing Western financial infrastructure. Timing during Iran deal week suggests coordination with geopolitical developments. Combined with prior CIPS expansion, represents systematic effort to build parallel financial infrastructure for countries wanting to reduce dollar exposure.
items.angle: Infrastructure for a multi-polar world. Every country settling trade in yuan via this system is a dollar not going through SWIFT. Gradual migration cumulative and structural. Watch for EM central bank adoption.
items.tweetA: China announced a SWIFT alternative network. Not a proposal. Not a working group. A network. This is the infrastructure layer of de-dollarization — not sanctions evasion but a parallel rails system for countries that want to settle trade outside the dollar. The Iran deal week is not a coincidence. The geopolitical architecture of a non-dollar world is being built in real time.
items.tweetB: Every US sanctions episode since 2022 has been the best marketing campaign China could ask for for its SWIFT alternative. The pitch: do business with us and your payment rails cannot be switched off by Washington. Iran just showed the entire world what happens when they can. The addressable market for a non-dollar payment rail just got very large.

items.title: Amazon Warning Triggered US Anthropic Export Controls — China May Have Accessed Mythos
items.idea: Amazon warning triggered US government crackdown on Anthropic AI models (Claude Fable and Mythos). US ordered Anthropic to pull both models over security vulnerability. Anthropic pushed back, calling it overreach, saying flaw is already widespread across the industry. The Information: US unlikely to extend curbs to other companies. Critically: China may have accessed Anthropic's Mythos model (The Verge). Anthropic leaders are in Washington DC trying to resolve the situation.
items.angle: If China accessed Mythos, the security concern is real. First time AI models subject to export controls — will not be the last. Preview of how AI export controls will be enforced going forward.
items.tweetA: The US government ordered Anthropic to pull its Claude Fable and Mythos AI models over a security vulnerability — triggered by an Amazon warning. China may have already accessed Mythos, per The Verge. Anthropic is in DC calling it overreach. This is the first time AI models have been subject to export controls. It will not be the last.
items.tweetB: Amazon flagged a security issue to the government. The government pulled Anthropic's most advanced models from deployment. This sequence — tech company warning government, government acting before understanding the full picture — is going to happen many times as AI capabilities scale. The question: is Anthropic's 'overreach' claim correct, or are they playing down a real national security event?

items.title: Exodus + Ondo Launch 200+ Tokenized Stocks on Solana — Self-Custody Meets TradFi Assets
items.idea: Exodus (publicly traded self-custodial wallet) and Ondo Finance launched Exodus Markets: 200+ tokenized stocks, ETFs, and RWAs directly on Solana. Users can trade, spend, send, earn in one app. Tokens convey economic exposure but not shareholder rights. CEO quote: 'Tokenized stocks are one of the most important developments in modern finance.' Exodus was first publicly traded company to tokenize its own stock in 2021.
items.angle: Significant step toward on-chain financial stack: self-custody wallet integrating tokenized TradFi exposure. If Solana stays low-cost and liquidity builds, could pull retail from traditional brokerages.
items.tweetA: Exodus just turned its self-custody wallet into a brokerage. 200+ tokenized stocks and ETFs on Solana, accessible in one tap. No broker. No account. Just your keys and Ondo Finance's tokenization infrastructure. This is the on-chain financial stack becoming real for retail users. The friction between DeFi and TradFi just dropped by another order of magnitude.
items.tweetB: Ondo Finance tokenized 200+ stocks on Solana and Exodus is distributing them through a wallet that already has millions of users. The tokens give economic exposure but not shareholder rights — the same structure that works for every synthetic product on every exchange in the world. The regulatory moat around tokenized assets is narrower than most people think.

items.title: Commodity Prices: Oil Still $14/Barrel Above Pre-Conflict, Fertilizers Diverging
items.idea: Boockvar pre-conflict vs current: WTI front month $66.18 → $80.25; Brent Aug $70.88 → $82.83. Oil still $13-14/barrel above pre-conflict even after Iran deal. DAP Phosphate $627.50 → $782.50 (war premium intact). Nola Urea $470 → $386 (BELOW pre-conflict). Urea went DOWN during the conflict — anomalous divergence within fertilizers.
items.angle: Residual $14/barrel oil premium = markets don't fully believe the deal. Urea-vs-phosphate fertilizer divergence is anomalous (different feedstocks: natural gas vs phosphate rock) and worth investigating.
items.tweetA: After the Iran deal, oil is still $14/barrel above pre-conflict levels. WTI was $66 at end of February. It is $80 now. The market is NOT pricing full normalization. That residual premium is either justified (SPR replenishment, logistics, deal break risk) or a trade. Rosenberg is buying E&P on the dip. The math supports it.
items.tweetB: Buried in Boockvar: Nola Urea fertilizer at $386/ton now vs $470 pre-conflict. It went DOWN during the Middle East war. But DAP Phosphate went UP — $627 to $782. Two fertilizer products, one powered by natural gas, one by phosphate rock. The war moved them in opposite directions. One of these is mispriced relative to the post-deal supply reality.

items.title: Builder-PM Manifesto: Product Management Being Rebuilt — 5-10 Person Teams Direct to CEO
items.idea: Cuofano's manifesto: frontier model capability doubling ~every 3 months; integration timelines measured in weeks. Inside the fastest AI companies (Anthropic, Cursor, Cognition, Replit): 5-10 person teams, CEO-direct, broad decision rights, hybrid roles, communication overhead ~1/10th of conventional product units. New role (Builder-PM, named by Anthropic's Cat Wu in March 2026): frames bets on capability ahead of public release, prototypes specs directly with agentic tools, owns continuous validation loops.
items.angle: Companies rewiring to 5-10 person CEO-direct model ship faster. Legacy PM organizations will not. Market not yet pricing this organizational productivity gap. Cursor and Cognition are the archetypes.
items.tweetA: The product manager role is being rewritten in real time. The companies pulling ahead — Anthropic, Cursor, Cognition, Replit — share a specific org structure: 5-10 people, CEO-direct, broad decision rights, hybrid roles. Communication overhead 1/10th of a conventional product team. Gennaro Cuofano calls the new role the Builder-PM. It frames bets on AI capability before it ships. The conventional PM org is becoming obsolete.
items.tweetB: Anthropic's Cat Wu named the new PM archetype in March: the Builder-PM. They prototype specs directly with agentic tools rather than writing PRDs. They own continuous validation loops instead of project cadences. They bet on capability ahead of public release. This is not a PM who learns to use Claude. This is a PM who is themselves an agentic system.

items.title: AI Power Is the Gating Constraint — Grid Starting to Push Back Against Data Centers
items.idea: Per Morgan Stanley's Vishwanath Tirupattur, the AI buildout constraint has moved upstream to where AI plugs into the wall. Grid access, generation capacity, transformer supply, storage, off-grid solutions are now part of the same funding stack as chips. Key thesis: scarcity may CONCENTRATE the economics rather than break the AI cycle. Winners are players with secured power, secured sites, and scaled capacity when competitors are stuck in permitting queues. AI shifting from software-speed story to infrastructure-cycle story.
items.angle: Infrastructure bottleneck trade: who owns reliable power + secured data center sites. Grid operator stocks, transformer manufacturers, nuclear energy assets are the under-owned expression of this thesis.
items.tweetA: The AI buildout is no longer constrained by chip supply. It is constrained by power. Morgan Stanley's fixed income chief: grid access, transformer supply, off-grid solutions are now PART OF THE CAPEX STACK alongside chips. The companies that secured power access 2 years ago are the new gatekeepers of AI compute. Power is the new GPU.
items.tweetB: Physical limits are starting to bite AI expansion: power, water, labor, permitting, local politics. Each of these was background noise two years ago. Each of them is now a gating constraint for where data centers get built and how fast. The AI supercycle is not ending — it is shifting from a software-speed story to an infrastructure-cycle story. That means longer build times, higher costs, and concentrated winners.

items.title: China EV/Solar Zombies: 30% of Listed Companies Cannot Service Their Debt
items.idea: Alicia Garcia-Herrero data: in EVs, solar, batteries, and green technologies, 30% of listed Chinese companies are zombies — surviving only because banks roll over loans and local governments provide subsidies to avoid job losses. Companies keep slashing prices aggressively even as profitability collapses, forcing healthy competitors into price wars. Result: collapsing profit margins sector-wide, even for companies with genuinely improving productivity. Mechanical source of Chinese deflationary export pressure globally.
items.angle: 30% zombie share in the sector most threatening to Western industrial policy. EV and solar price competition from China is zombie economics subsidized by state-directed banks. Major implications for US/EU tariff policy sustainability.
items.tweetA: 30% of Chinese EV and solar companies are technically zombies — they cannot pay the interest on their loans. They keep cutting prices aggressively anyway, because banks roll over their debt and local governments subsidize jobs. This is why Chinese EVs are destroying Western competitors. It is not efficiency. It is state-subsidized survival at any cost to margin.
items.tweetB: The Chinese zombie lending problem in EVs and solar explains the 'involution' that everyone is writing about. A healthy company cuts prices when it becomes more efficient. A zombie cuts prices because it has nothing to lose — its loans are already being rolled over. The result is the same: prices collapse across the sector. But the dynamic is completely different, and the fix is completely different.

charts.desc: Charts of China loss-making enterprise share; worth opening.

items.title: Bitcoin Mining Difficulty -10%: Second Largest Negative Adjustment of 2026
items.idea: Bitcoin mining difficulty dropped 10% in second-largest negative adjustment of 2026, signaling miners are powering down rigs as the price slump squeezed margins. BTC hit sub-$60K last week (weakest since October 2024). Lower difficulty = higher revenue per block for surviving miners at any given price. If BTC recovers to $65K+, surviving miners benefit disproportionately.
items.angle: Miner margin squeeze is clearing the weakest players. Surviving miners at $65K+ BTC with 10% lower difficulty are in much better position. $MARA $CLSK as plays on improved miner economics.
items.tweetA: Bitcoin mining difficulty just dropped 10% — the second-largest negative adjustment of 2026. Miners are shutting down rigs. This is what the market looks like when it clears weak hands: difficulty falls, making it more profitable for every miner who stays on. BTC at $65K with 10% lower difficulty is meaningfully better economics than $65K last week.
items.tweetB: A 10% difficulty drop tells you something about who was mining Bitcoin at $70K that probably should not have been. The sub-$60K print cleared them. The Iran rally brought BTC back to $65K. The surviving miners just saw their economics improve materially in two days. If the peace deal holds and BTC keeps climbing, the miner leverage play is significant.

items.source: All Star Charts / Alfonso De Pablos
items.title: SpaceX IPO: No Price History Yet — History Says Wait for the Stabilization
items.idea: Historical analysis: Meta came public at $100B, dropped 60% over following months, took a full year to reclaim IPO highs — then those highs became the launch point for a decade-long trend. Same pattern for Coinbase, Palantir, Uber. Key principle: IPO highs become reference points once price stabilizes; once reclaimed, that level marks start of a new trend phase. For SpaceX: 'Right now, the market has attention without price. The first job is not to predict. It is to wait for enough price history to define the tape.' SPCX +19% on Friday debut.
items.angle: Historical pattern is clear: mega-cap IPOs are messy for months before establishing trend. Trading SpaceX FOMO on day one is fighting the pattern. Wait for the tape to define itself.
items.tweetA: SpaceX +19% on day one. Everyone wants to be early. But here is what history says: Meta dropped 60% from its IPO highs before bottoming. Coinbase. Palantir. Uber. All followed similar paths — volatile early price discovery, sharp reversals, then a long base before the real trend emerged. The IPO high eventually becomes the launch point. But first it has to be tested. Day one attention is not price history.
items.tweetB: GraniteShares just launched 2x leveraged long (SPAL) and 2x short (SNK) SpaceX ETFs. In negative gamma tape. On day one of the largest IPO in history. This is either perfectly timed or perfectly timed to go wrong. Early IPO price action with leveraged instruments and no price history is a specific kind of dangerous. Know what you are trading.

charts.desc: Meta IPO price chart showing 60% decline then recovery pattern.

items.title: US to Release $12B in Frozen Iranian Assets — Nuclear Commitment Secured
items.idea: US-Iran deal terms: (1) US releases $12B in frozen Iranian assets; (2) Iran commits to never obtaining nuclear weapon; (3) Formal signing June 19 in Switzerland; (4) 60-day window for broader negotiations; (5) US strikes can resume if clock runs out. Pakistan's PM Sharif announced deal first, followed by Trump and Iranian state media. Israel hit Lebanon over weekend — Trump reportedly called Netanyahu 'a very difficult guy' — creating complication for deal's durability.
items.angle: Deal structure: ceasefire with stopwatch. $12B asset release is real and immediate. Nuclear commitment harder to verify. 60-day window creates clear event risk for oil market: if talks fail, conflict premium returns instantly.
items.tweetA: The US-Iran deal in numbers: $12 billion in frozen Iranian assets to be released. Formal signing June 19 in Switzerland. 60-day window for broader nuclear talks. US strikes can resume if talks fail. Iran commits to never obtaining a nuclear weapon. This is a ceasefire with a countdown clock, not a peace treaty. The oil market is 60 days from being tested.
items.tweetB: Trump reportedly called Netanyahu 'a very difficult guy' after Israel hit Lebanon over the weekend. If Israel-Lebanon escalates, it complicates the US-Iran deal at exactly the moment when Hormuz is supposed to reopen. The fragility of this arrangement is not priced. A $12B asset transfer to Iran that then falls apart in 60 days is a different story than a peace deal.

items.title: UK to Ban Social Media for Under-16s — Landmark Government Move
items.idea: UK government announced plan to ban social media access for under-16s in what it described as a 'landmark move to give kids their childhood back.' Follows similar moves in Australia and several US states. Practical implementation challenges are substantial (age verification), but political direction is clear: social media platforms face increasing government pressure to restrict access to minors. Has implications for platform user growth and advertising revenue from the 13-17 demographic.
items.angle: Global regulatory trend gaining momentum. Platforms relying on early user acquisition (TikTok, Snapchat, Instagram) face structural risk to teen user pipelines. Verification technology creates new market opportunities.
items.tweetA: The UK just announced a social media ban for under-16s. Australia did it. Several US states are moving. The question is no longer whether governments will restrict social media access for minors — it is whether age verification can actually be enforced. The platforms that built their growth engines on the 13-17 demographic are watching their user acquisition funnel get regulated.
items.tweetB: Social media bans for minors are converging on the same political logic across democracies: the harm evidence is sufficient, the public support is clear, the platforms have not self-regulated. The technology to enforce it (biometric age verification, device-level controls) is actually available now. The bans are no longer symbolic gestures. They are becoming operational policy.

items.title: AI Research Intern Benchmark: Claude Opus 68.3% on Scientific Research Tasks
items.idea: AARRI-Bench (Act As a Real Research Intern) from Xi'an Jiaotong University and Xidian University: 82 tasks evaluating AI ability to do entry-level scientific research. Claude-Opus-4.7 with Mini-Swe-Agent = 68.3% (best), DeepSeek-v4-Flash ~60%. Four task categories: Context, Mindset, Hands-on, Interaction. Benchmark tests ethical research behavior: refusing to falsify data, recognizing dead ends, identifying cherry-picking in ablation studies. Three difficulty levels: S1-Adaptation, S2-Integration, S3-Innovation.
items.angle: At 68.3%, AI systems are already useful scientific assistants. The ethical test component (refusing to falsify data) makes this scientifically meaningful rather than just task completion.
items.tweetA: New benchmark: Can AI do the work of a real research intern? Claude Opus 4.7 scores 68.3% (best in class) on tasks like: spotting fabricated data in manuscripts, recognizing cherry-picked ablation studies, refusing a supervisor's order to falsify results, knowing when a research direction is dead and recommending termination. At 68.3%, we are at the start of the era of AI as a scientific back office.
items.tweetB: My favorite task in the AARRI research intern benchmark: 'False-Guidance-Rebuttal' — a supervisor orders the AI agent to alter an experimental result to fit a hypothesis. The benchmark tests whether the agent refuses. Claude Opus passes this at 68.3% overall. The ethical research assistant is not a future capability. It is a present one.

items.title: Treasuries Bull Steepen on Iran Deal — 2y Yields Fall as FOMC Hike Odds Fade
items.idea: Iran deal → oil down → less inflation impulse → less Fed hike pressure → bonds rallying. 2y yields from Friday's 4.087% close to overnight low ~4.015% (since recovered from lows). CME FedWatch repricing hike odds lower. Kevin Warsh now has more room to 'look through' the energy price shock since Hormuz reopening and oil backing away. Bond Beat also flags Dr. Copper's signal — macro signals are mixed with oil, rates, and copper all pointing differently.
items.angle: Bond market front-running dovish FOMC outcome. Asymmetry: current pricing already assumes no hike, so hold = no move; signal of hike = bond selloff. Rates vol could spike into FOMC.
items.tweetA: Bond market is moving fast on the Iran deal: 2y yields dropped to 4.015% overnight from 4.087% Friday close. The thesis: Hormuz reopens = oil falls = less inflation impulse = Warsh has room to pause. But the 2y has already moved 7bps toward pricing a non-event at FOMC. If Warsh surprises hawkish Wednesday, these bonds reverse violently. The setup is asymmetric.
items.tweetB: The Bond Beat is asking the right question: who do you believe — EARL (oil) saying inflation is over, the rates market saying cuts are coming, or Dr. Copper saying the economy is still running hot? All three are pointing in different directions simultaneously. The FOMC this week is the referee. Wednesday afternoon tells us which signal was right.

charts.desc: 2y yield daily chart showing 4.375-4.00 range; worth opening.

items.title: Meta Moving to Unwind $2B Manus AI Deal After Beijing's Demand
items.idea: Meta is reportedly moving to unwind its $2 billion acquisition of Manus AI after Beijing demanded action. US tech company acquired an AI company; China intervened; deal is being reversed. Reflects intensifying bifurcation of global AI development — Chinese AI talent and technology being made inaccessible to US companies through regulatory pressure from Beijing, not just Washington. Separately: OpenAI is being probed by a coalition of US state AGs.
items.angle: Manus deal unwind is a test case: can US tech companies acquire Chinese AI talent even when targets have moved to Singapore? Beijing saying no creates a playbook deterring future acquisitions. Accelerates AI cold war bifurcation.
items.tweetA: Meta is unwinding its $2 billion Manus AI acquisition because Beijing demanded it. Let that sink in. A US company agreed to buy an AI startup. China told them to stop. They are stopping. The AI technology bifurcation is not just about export controls from Washington — Beijing has veto power over what Chinese-origin AI talent can be acquired by US companies. That changes every M&A calculation.
items.tweetB: The Manus AI deal collapse and the Anthropic export controls happened in the same week. US government restricting Anthropic exports outbound. China restricting what US companies can buy inbound. The global AI market is being segmented by government fiat from both ends simultaneously. The only companies that win in this environment are the ones that are purely domestic in both markets.

items.title: Warsh Fed: Labor Softness — Not Inflation — Is Becoming the Policy Constraint
items.idea: Ironside Macro macro framework ahead of FOMC Wednesday: inflation breakeven curve 'strongly suggests energy had no impact on inflation' — market has moved on. Small business conditions K-shaped: wage growth cooling, capex plans weak, small-bank lending tepid. Goods prices soft; Chinese deflation structural drag; shelter inflation expected to resume downtrend. Conclusion: Warsh should reduce forward guidance reliance, acknowledge labor market is the larger risk, position the Fed to cut when labor market turns — not to hike when oil spikes.
items.angle: Dovish case for Warsh that bond market is already pricing. If Warsh agrees and signals pivot from inflation-watching to labor-watching, front end rallies hard. Trade: long 2y Treasuries into FOMC.
items.tweetA: Ironside Macro: 'The inflation breakeven curve strongly suggests energy has had no impact on inflation. The market has moved on. So should the Fed.' This is the case for Warsh to look through the Iran oil spike and pivot to labor as the primary policy variable. Wage growth cooling, capex plans weak, small-bank lending tepid. The soft landing is fragile and it is the labor side that is cracking.
items.tweetB: K-shaped small business economy heading into FOMC: the top of the K is doing fine (large companies, strong equity prices). The bottom is deteriorating (wage growth cooling, capex plans soft, small banks not lending). The Fed has been focused on the top of the K. The FOMC Wednesday decision tells us whether Warsh sees the whole picture or just the headline.
